title: Chapter 5 summary and quiz
fixes_applied: MAJ-2: Ex2 -- replaced bitwise AND exercise with a non-bitwise exercise. The new exercise tests constexpr, string_view, and if/else (all Ch 5 material) without requiring bitwise operators (Ch 6) or ternary (Ch 6).
MAJ-3: Ex2 -- eliminated ternary operator usage by redesigning the exercise entirely.

exercises.title: Classify a String with constexpr Length Thresholds
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the program that reads a word from stdin and classifies it by length using `constexpr` thresholds and a `std::string_view` parameter, printing the classification.

## Input
- Line 1: a single word (no spaces, 1 to 50 characters)

## Output
Print exactly two lines:
1. `Word: <word> (length: <N>)` -- where `<word>` is the input and `<N>` is its length
2. One of:
   - `Category: short` if length <= SHORT_MAX (3)
   - `Category: medium` if length <= MEDIUM_MAX (7) and length > SHORT_MAX
   - `Category: long` if length > MEDIUM_MAX

## Requirements
1. Declare `constexpr int SHORT_MAX = 3;` and `constexpr int MEDIUM_MAX = 7;` at namespace scope using named constants (not magic numbers).
2. Write a function `void classify(std::string_view word)` that accepts the word as `std::string_view`, prints both output lines, and uses the two `constexpr` thresholds with `if`/`else if`/`else` to determine the category.
3. Read the word into a `std::string` in `main` and pass it to `classify` (testing implicit conversion from `std::string` to `std::string_view`).
4. Use `word.length()` to get the size -- do not compute length manually.
5. Edge cases covered by hidden tests: a 1-character word (short), a 3-character word (short boundary), a 7-character word (medium boundary), and a long word.

## Notes
- Allowed headers: `<iostream>`, `<string>`, `<string_view>`.
